metadata.title: How Music Works (Byrne) — Book Review

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Bach and Mozart composed for specific rooms, whose echo and size shaped the music itself. Byrne argues context comes first, so venues and technology determine what music becomes. Edison's early cylinders were fragile and mechanical, forcing bands to replay songs repeatedly. Recording technology changed not just how we hear music but what music fundamentally is.

props.artifactTitle: How Music Works - space shapes sound
artifactLines: Bach and Mozart composed for specific rooms, whose echo and size shaped the music itself.
Byrne argues context comes first, so venues and technology determine what music becomes.
Edison's early cylinders were fragile and mechanical, forcing bands to replay songs repeatedly.
Recording technology changed not just how we hear music but what music fundamentally is.

beats.narration_text: The claim that Bach wrote for specific room echoes and Mozart for palace chambers, not symphony halls, reframed music as shaped by its space. Byrne's Edison cylinders pushed that further. Show me: (1) how a room's reverberation time would push a composer toward slower or sparser writing, (2) why bands re-performing songs for each cylinder batch changed what a recording even was, and (3) one modern song whose arrangement was clearly built for headphones rather than a hall.
